notable.notable_blurb: The greatest poet of Puritan America

cross_connections.display_label: married his daughter Keziah, then a Hooker descendant

narrative_blocks.header: He is reckoned the finest Puritan poet
narrative_blocks.body: Edward Taylor wrote intricate devotional poetry in the metaphysical vein but forbade its publication in his lifetime. His manuscripts were discovered at Yale in 1937, and he is now regarded as the greatest poet of Puritan America.

narrative_blocks.header: He kept one frontier pulpit fifty-eight years
narrative_blocks.body: A Harvard graduate of 1671, Taylor ministered to the frontier town of Westfield, Massachusetts, for fifty-eight years and doctored his congregation besides. He had left Leicestershire in 1668 rather than conform to the English church.

narrative_blocks.header: His grandson became president of Yale
narrative_blocks.body: Taylor's daughter Keziah married Rev. Isaac Stiles, and their son Ezra Stiles rose to the presidency of Yale. Through Isaac Stiles's later marriage to Esther Hooker, Taylor's family brushed the line of Thomas Hooker.

parents.mother_research_notes: Ruth Wyllys (daughter of Hon. Samuel Wyllys of Hartford)

I00126.gravestone_inscription: This monument is erected to the memory of the Rev. Isaac Stiles, A.M., who was born in Windsor, July 30th, 1697, received a liberal education at Yale College; ordained to the pastoral office in the church of North Haven, November 11th, 1724, where he served in the ministry 36 years, and died May 14th, 1760, aged 63. ...he preached the gospel with fervor and fidelity. A friend to pure and undefiled religion, with a charitable benevolence to all mankind. Mors Mihi vita Est.

cross_connections.display_label: was father of his first wife, Keziah

narrative_blocks.header: Thirty-six years in North Haven’s pulpit
narrative_blocks.body: Yale 1722, Stiles held the North Haven pastorate for thirty-six years and raised — by his first wife — Ezra Stiles, the Enlightenment polymath who became Yale’s seventh president. His Hooker-descended children came by his second wife, Esther Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: He preached against his own melon thief
narrative_blocks.body: Spotting a parishioner stealing his melons during the Sunday intermission, Stiles named him from the afternoon pulpit: “I saw you, John Johnson, thou son of Belial… enter my garden and steal my melons.” His son Ezra called it “rather close preaching, but characteristic of the times.”

narrative_blocks.header: His first wife was the poet’s daughter
narrative_blocks.body: Keziah Taylor, daughter of the metaphysical poet Rev. Edward Taylor of Westfield, died in childbed in December 1727 — twenty-five years old, two years married. Within the year Stiles remarried, to Esther Hooker of Farmington, who bore his ten North Haven children.

narrative_blocks.header: First of his blood liberally educated
narrative_blocks.body: Yale 1722 — “the first of the name and blood that had a liberal education in America” — he published “A Looking-glass for Changlings” against political turncoats in 1743. His son Ezra became president of the college; his epitaph fills a monument at North Haven.

cross_connections.display_label: Yale classicist James Luce Kingsley wrote his biography
Held dying friend Chauncey Whittlesey's hand for his last communication
Eulogized Whittlesey at his 1787 Yale deathbed
cross_connections.notes: Ezra Stiles (Yale president 1778-1795) attended Rev. Chauncey Whittlesey's deathbed in 1787 and eulogized him: 'He was an excellent classical scholar, well acquainted with three learned languages...amassed by reading a great treasure of wisdom.' Per Whittlesey Genealogy #88.

cross_connections.display_label: was his grandfather, the Westfield poet

institutions.institution_blurb: Yale College B.A. 1746, M.A. 1749. Tutor 1749-1753. 7th President 1778-1795. Died in office. Required all students to study Hebrew; delivered his first commencement address in Hebrew, Aramaic, and Arabic.
Co-founder of Brown University (then Rhode Island College). Drafted the College's first Charter in 1763; revised version adopted by Rhode Island General Assembly March 3, 1764.

narrative_blocks.header: His mother died four days after he was
narrative_blocks.body: Ezra Stiles was born in North Haven on December 10, 1727. His mother Kezia Taylor Stiles died four days later. His father Rev. Isaac Stiles remarried Esther Hooker, granddaughter of Thomas Hooker — making the Yale president, Brown University co-founder, and Enlightenment figure a stepson of the Hooker line.

narrative_blocks.header: He sent rum on a slave voyage
narrative_blocks.body: In 1756, Stiles invested in a slave voyage by sending rum to Africa; he received back a 10-year-old boy he renamed Newport. That same year, Stiles and fellow Newport minister Samuel Hopkins wrote a joint public letter condemning 'the great inhumanity and cruelty' of slavery in the United States. He freed Newport in 1778 when he left for Yale — then in 1782 re-indentured him and his wife for $20 a year, and separately bound Newport's two-year-old son until the boy turned 24.

narrative_blocks.header: He personally drafted the charter of Brown University
narrative_blocks.body: During his Newport years, Stiles drafted petitions, wrote letters, and ultimately authored the Charter of what became Brown University, adopted by the Rhode Island General Assembly on March 3, 1764. He envisioned a nonsectarian college where major religious groups could pursue knowledge together. When Baptists amended his charter to take control of the College's Corporation, Stiles refused a seat on the board, writing bitterly that they had seized 'the whole Power and Government of the College.'

narrative_blocks.header: He shocked 52 people with static electricity
narrative_blocks.body: As Yale's president, Stiles became its first professor of Semitics and conducted electrical experiments using equipment donated by Benjamin Franklin. He charged a glass tube and shocked 52 people at once, fired spirits of wine and rum, and animated counterfeit spiders. He also corresponded with Thomas Jefferson and George Washington about scientific discoveries.

education.notes: — Yale President 1778-1795. NOT a Hooker descendant by blood — born to Rev. Isaac Stiles's first wife BEFORE Isaac married Esther Hooker (H00092) in October 1728. Raised from infancy by his stepmother Esther Hooker, Thomas Hooker's great-granddaughter. Half-brother of H00238 Isaac Stiles Jr. and siblings.

parents.father_research_notes: First Governor of Connecticut; sailed with Thomas Hooker 1633.

INST002.name: Harvard College
INST002.notes: Rev. Thomas Shepard (I00002) was instrumental in founding Harvard and placing it at Cambridge. Rev. Samuel Shepard (H00008) graduated 1658; Samuel Shepard grandson (H00038) graduated 1685; Col. Roger Newton (H00044) also a graduate.

INST022.primary_name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.name: American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions
INST022.notes: First American foreign-mission organization. Tree connections: Rev. Elias Cornelius (agent 1816, secretary 1831-32); Ainsworth and Harriet Ellsworth Blunt, missionaries to the Cherokee at Brainerd, Tennessee; Eliza Talcott, missionary to Japan; Rev. William Banfield Capron, Madura mission; Judge John Hooker, member.
